content.text: The ART Topology for GenAI Programmes

An Agile Release Train (ART) is SAFe's primary organisational unit: a long-lived team of agile teams (typically 50–125 people) that plan, commit, and deliver together on a PI cadence. Most enterprise GenAI programmes span more than one ART because the work naturally partitions into distinct value streams — model development, platform infrastructure, and user-facing features — each with different backlogs, skill sets, and delivery rhythms.
A programme with multiple ARTs is a Solution Train. The Lean-Agile Centre of Excellence (LACE) or a Solution Train Engineer coordinates across ARTs while each ART retains autonomous planning.

attrs.alt: ART Coordination Topology for a GenAI Programme

content.text: The diagram above shows a three-ART topology common for mid-scale GenAI programmes. The key structural insight is that ART 1 (Foundation Models) outputs
that ART 2 (Platform) depends on, which in turn exposes a
that ART 3 (Product) builds against. Both dependency chains must be coordinated explicitly — neither is visible from a single ART's backlog.

A model contract is the formal interface agreement between the team that produces a model and the teams that consume it. It specifies the model version, endpoint, input schema, output schema, latency SLA, and quality threshold (eval metric + minimum passing score). Registering contracts centrally prevents consumer teams from discovering breaking changes mid-sprint.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class ContractStatus(Enum):
    DRAFT = "draft"
    ACTIVE = "active"
    DEPRECATED = "deprecated"


@dataclass
class ModelContract:
    model_id: str
    version: str
    provider_art: str             # ART that owns the model
    endpoint: str
    input_schema_ref: str         # JSON Schema URI or inline dict key
    output_schema_ref: str
    latency_sla_p95_ms: int
    eval_metric: str              # e.g. "ROUGE-L"
    eval_threshold: float         # minimum passing score
    valid_from: date
    deprecation_date: Optional[date] = None
    status: ContractStatus = ContractStatus.DRAFT
    consumers: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def is_active_on(self, d: date) -> bool:
        if self.status != ContractStatus.ACTIVE:
            return False
        if self.deprecation_date and d >= self.deprecation_date:
            return False
        return d >= self.valid_from

    def register_consumer(self, art_name: str) -> None:
        if art_name not in self.consumers:
            self.consumers.append(art_name)

    def deprecation_warning(self, current_date: date, warning_days: int = 30) -> Optional[str]:
        if self.deprecation_date is None:
            return None
        days_left = (self.deprecation_date - current_date).days
        if 0 < days_left <= warning_days:
            return (
                f"CONTRACT EXPIRY WARNING: {self.model_id} v{self.version} "
                f"deprecates in {days_left} days — notify consumers: {self.consumers}"
            )
        return None


# Registry (in practice: a shared database or config repo)
registry: dict[str, ModelContract] = {}

contract = ModelContract(
    model_id="summariser-v2",
    version="2.3.1",
    provider_art="Foundation Models ART",
    endpoint="/api/v1/summarise",
    input_schema_ref="schemas/summarise-request.json",
    output_schema_ref="schemas/summarise-response.json",
    latency_sla_p95_ms=2000,
    eval_metric="ROUGE-L",
    eval_threshold=0.42,
    valid_from=date(2026, 7, 1),
    deprecation_date=date(2026, 12, 31),
    status=ContractStatus.ACTIVE,
)
contract.register_consumer("Product Features ART")
registry[f"{contract.model_id}:{contract.version}"] = contract

warn = contract.deprecation_warning(date(2026, 12, 10))
if warn:
    print(warn)

Consumer ARTs register themselves on the contract at PI Planning. When the provider ART updates or deprecates a model, they notify all registered consumers before changing the contract status — eliminating the silent-breaking-change pattern that causes mid-sprint integration failures.
The Scrum-of-Scrums runs weekly across ARTs, attended by each ART's RTE and nominated team representatives. For GenAI programmes the SoS agenda has four standing items:
Any contracts that changed version, latency, or eval score this week
Consumer teams notified if breaking change
Escalate to Solution Train if unresolved in 48 h
Actual vs. allocated GPU-hours by ART through current sprint
Rebalance if any ART at > 85% with sprints remaining
Traffic-light status for next integration milestone
AMBER/RED triggers joint sprint planning session

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum


class RAG(Enum):
    GREEN = "GREEN"
    AMBER = "AMBER"
    RED = "RED"


@dataclass
class SoSAgendaItem:
    category: str
    art: str
    description: str
    status: RAG
    action: str = ""
    owner: str = ""

    def needs_escalation(self) -> bool:
        return self.status == RAG.RED


@dataclass
class SoSMeeting:
    sprint: int
    items: list[SoSAgendaItem] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add_item(self, item: SoSAgendaItem) -> None:
        self.items.append(item)

    def escalations(self) -> list[SoSAgendaItem]:
        return [it for it in self.items if it.needs_escalation()]

    def print_summary(self) -> None:
        print(f"SoS — Sprint {self.sprint} Summary")
        for it in self.items:
            flag = "🔴" if it.status == RAG.RED else ("🟡" if it.status == RAG.AMBER else "🟢")
            print(f"  {flag} [{it.art}] {it.category}: {it.description}")
            if it.action:
                print(f"       → {it.action} ({it.owner})")
        escs = self.escalations()
        if escs:
            print(f"\n  {len(escs)} item(s) escalated to Solution Train.")


meeting = SoSMeeting(sprint=3)
meeting.add_item(SoSAgendaItem(
    category="Model Contract",
    art="Foundation Models ART",
    description="summariser-v2 latency degraded to 2 800 ms p95 — SLA breach",
    status=RAG.RED,
    action="Hotfix sprint started; consumer teams switch to summariser-v1 as fallback",
    owner="Foundation Models RTE",
))
meeting.add_item(SoSAgendaItem(
    category="Compute Quota",
    art="Product Features ART",
    description="API token spend at 91% of PI budget by sprint 3 of 5",
    status=RAG.AMBER,
    action="Reduce A/B test traffic from 20% to 10% until PI end",
    owner="Product RTE",
))
meeting.print_summary()

When multiple ARTs each run their own evaluation suites, metric drift occurs: the same model appears to improve on one ART's tests while regressing on another's, because the test data or thresholds diverged. A shared, centrally governed eval suite eliminates this.
— the eval suite lives in a shared repository owned by the platform or data-science guild. All ARTs reference the same test sets and metric implementations.
— the suite is frozen at the start of each PI. No changes to test cases, metrics, or datasets are made until I&A; change requests are queued in a backlog.
— every model evaluation run is stored with the eval-suite version as a tag. This makes it possible to compare results across PIs using the same suite version.
— each ART runs the suite at the start of each sprint on any model version they depend on. Results are posted to the SoS channel so all ARTs see the same quality signal.
Pattern 4: Integration Milestones as Coordination Gates

Integration milestones (IMs) are synchronisation points where the outputs of multiple ARTs must be integrated and validated together before the programme proceeds. GenAI programmes typically have two or three IMs per PI:
All consumer ARTs confirm they can build against the frozen API
Eval suite pass + latency SLA met on staging environment
All acceptance criteria met; go/no-go from Programme Manager

— a provider ART updates a model without updating the registry or notifying consumers. Consumer ARTs discover the change when eval scores shift unexpectedly in sprint 2. Fix: make contract updates a pull-request process with mandatory consumer review.

— teams request maximum budget at PI Planning to avoid mid-PI shortfalls. This inflates the total envelope and leaves no buffer for the programme. Fix: start with bottom-up objective-based budgets; add a 15% programme-level buffer managed by the platform team.
Integration milestones treated as optional sync points

— when IMs slip because "we'll catch up," the gate criterion is never enforced and late integration failures hit production. Fix: missing an IM triggers an immediate escalation to the Solution Train, not a quiet slip.
— each ART maintains its own test set with different metric versions. Model quality appears to improve overall but regresses in production. Fix: shared eval suite governance with PI-level freeze.
Coordinating Across Frameworks: SAFe, LeSS, and Scrum@Scale

When a GenAI programme spans teams using different agile frameworks — common in acquisitions or multi-vendor delivery — the coordination patterns above still apply, but the ceremony names and cadences differ:
ART dependency board at PI Planning
Definition of Done at Overall Sprint Review

The framework names differ but the coordination information is the same: who depends on what model contract, are integration gates passing, and is compute budget tracking to plan. Any cross-framework programme should establish a single coordination forum (equivalent to SoS) and a single eval suite, regardless of which framework each ART uses internally.
Operational Metrics for ART Coordination Health
Coordination patterns are only effective if their health is measured. Three metrics give a reliable signal that cross-ART coordination is working — or breaking down.

Count the number of times per PI that a consumer ART is broken by a model or API change it was not notified about in advance. A healthy programme targets zero; any breach warrants a post-incident review. Track it as a rate per 100 integration events so it can be compared across PIs of different lengths.

At the end of each sprint, compare actual GPU-hour consumption to the PI allocation plan. Variance > 15% in either direction is a coordination signal: high variance means teams are consuming outside their plan (scope creep or unexpected experiment costs); low variance means teams may be artificially throttling work to fit budgets rather than delivering value.

Track the percentage of integration milestones that pass their gate criteria by the planned sprint. An IM that slips by more than one sprint typically indicates that cross-ART dependency coordination failed earlier in the PI — the IM is the symptom, not the cause.
These four metrics together form a

that RTEs present at each I&A workshop. A programme with all four in green is coordinating effectively; amber or red metrics identify the specific failure mode — contract governance, resource planning, integration discipline, or escalation speed.
Scaling the Patterns: From 2 ARTs to 6+

The patterns above are described for a three-ART topology, which is the most common for GenAI programmes with a headcount of 100–300 engineers. As programmes scale beyond this, two structural adjustments are needed:

— beyond three ARTs the informal coordination that a single SoS can handle becomes insufficient. Formalise the Solution Train with a dedicated Solution Train Engineer (STE), a Solution Backlog, and a Solution Demo at the end of each PI. The STE role is the escalation point for cross-ART issues that the SoS cannot resolve within 48 hours.

— when there are many model-producing teams, a single flat registry becomes difficult to navigate. Partition it by domain (NLP, vision, tabular) or by business unit, with each sub-registry owned by a platform guild chapter. Consumer teams subscribe to the sub-registries relevant to their domain.
Federated eval suites with a canonical core

— at scale, teams need some domain-specific test cases that do not belong in a shared suite. Allow teams to maintain local eval extensions, but require that the shared canonical suite is always run as the baseline. Only canonical suite results are reported at the SoS; local extensions are the team's own quality bar.

— at 6+ ARTs, a flat SoS with all ART representatives becomes too large. Introduce a two-level structure: ART-level SoS meetings (team reps only) that feed into a Programme-level SoS (RTEs only) that feeds into the Solution Train review (STEs and Solution Architect).

The scaling adjustments share a principle: coordination information must flow upward when it cannot be resolved at the current level, and it must be resolved at the lowest level possible to avoid bottlenecks at the Solution Train.
Agile Release Train coordination for GenAI programmes rests on four patterns:
— formal versioned interface agreements that consumer ARTs register against, with mandatory notification before deprecation.
— weekly forum with four standing GenAI items: model contract status, cross-ART blockers, compute quota, and integration milestone readiness.
— a single centrally owned test suite, frozen per PI, that all ARTs reference to produce comparable quality signals.
— synchronisation points with enforceable pass/fail criteria that prevent late-integration failures from reaching production.

These patterns are framework-agnostic and apply whether the programme uses SAFe, LeSS, Scrum@Scale, or a mixture. The critical discipline is making the dependencies, quality signals, and resource constraints visible at the programme level rather than leaving them implicit within individual ARTs.
